Article.ArticleTitle: A green chemistry route to chitin nanocrystals and nanofibers using steam explosion and organic acid hydrolysis.

Abstract.AbstractText: This study reports a green and sustainable approach for producing chitin nanocrystals (ChNCs) and nanofibers (ChNFs) from shrimp shell chitin by integrating steam explosion pretreatment with citric acid hydrolysis. Citric acid was chosen as an eco-friendly alternative to strong mineral acids due to its low environmental impact and potential for recovery and reuse through rotary evaporation, aligning with circular economy principles. The steam explosion treatment was performed for 30 min and repeated for one to five cycles, each involving a rapid release of pressure, to examine the effect of successive cycles on nanochitin yield and properties. Citric acid selectively hydrolyzed the amorphous regions of chitin, producing 25.1% ChNCs after five cycles. The residual fraction was subjected to 25 min of ultrasonication to obtain ChNFs, yielding 67.3% after five cycles. An inverse relationship was observed, with ChNC yield increasing (18.5% to 25.1%) and ChNF yield decreasing (72.3% to 67.3%) across cycles. Morphologically, ChNCs were rod-shaped, with lengths decreasing from 388.06 nm (first cycle) to 297.17 nm (fifth cycle) and widths remaining similar (25.55-30.89 nm), while ChNFs formed fine, web-like networks with widths decreasing from 41.20 nm to 35.07 nm; their extensive micron-scale lengths could not be precisely measured. FTIR confirmed esterified carboxylic groups (1718 cm-1), indicating successful carboxylation, which imparted negative zeta potentials (-42.83 to -58.00 mV) and enhanced colloidal stability. XRD showed increased crystallinity indices, while TGA revealed comparable or improved thermal stability. This approach offers a facile, scalable, and environmentally benign route for producing functional chitin nanomaterials, supporting waste valorization and circular bioeconomy.
